epigraph.text:
For everything there is a season, and a time for 
every matter under heaven.
epigraph.quote: Algebra is the intellectual instrument which has been created for rendering clear the quantitative aspects of the world.

content.text: {Algebra is the intellectual instrument which has been created for rendering clear the quantitative aspects of the world.}{Alfred North Whitehead}

Just as Chapter 7 revealed special productsâ€”patterns in 
multiplicationâ€”this section reveals their reversals. Recognizing 
these patterns allows instant factoring without the AC method or 
trial and error.
Recall that $(a + b)(a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. Reversing this:

content.title: Difference of Squares
content.content:
\[
 a^2 - b^2 = (a + b)(a - b)
\]
A difference of two perfect squares factors as the sum and difference 
of their square roots.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
A **sum** of squares, $a^2 + b^2$, does **not** factor 
over the real numbers. There are no real numbers whose product is 
positive and whose squares add to give another square. Only 
*differences* of squares factor.

content.text: To recognize a perfect square trinomial, check:

items: First and last terms are perfect squares
Middle term is $\pm 2 \times$ (square root of first) $\times$ (square root of last)

content.text: These patterns are less common in Algebra 1 but worth knowing:

content.title: Remembering Cube Formulas
content.content:
Mnemonic: **SOAP**

- **S**ame sign: $(a \boxplus b)$

- **O**pposite sign: $(a^2 \boxminus ab$

- **A**lways **P**ositive: $+ b^2)$

content.text: Sometimes you must apply multiple patterns or factor out a GCF first.

marginNotes.text:
A time to weep and a time to laugh, 
a time to mourn and a time to dance.

The inner sanctuary was twenty cubits 
long, twenty wide and twenty high.

marginNotes.author: Pierre-Simon Laplace
marginNotes.text:
Nature laughs at the difficulties 
of integration.

marginNotes.author: Isaac Newton
marginNotes.text:
If I have seen further, it is by standing 
on the shoulders of giants.
